title: Tiramisu
subtitle: Italian espresso cream dessert with mascarpone and Marsala.
excerpt: Sponge fingers soaked in espresso and Marsala layered with a rich mascarpone cream of egg yolks and whipped whites, dusted with cocoa and chilled overnight. The classic Italian dessert. Serves six to eight.
sourceNotes: Tiramisu. The dessert originates in the Veneto region of northern Italy, with competing claims between Le Beccherie restaurant in Treviso (1960s) and other establishments. The name means 'pick me up', referring to the espresso and sugar. The mascarpone-and-zabaglione style became internationally popular from the 1980s onward. Standard versions appear in Italian cookbooks from the 1980s including those by Giorgio Locatelli.

content.text: Use savoiardi sponge fingers (also sold as ladyfingers or boudoir biscuits) for this recipe. They are light and porous, built to soak up espresso without falling apart. The recipe lists digestive biscuits as the closest match in this catalogue. Buy savoiardi from any Italian deli or the biscuit aisle of most supermarkets.

The egg whites give the cream lightness; do not skip them. Fold gently after whisking, overworking collapses the volume. The overnight chill is not optional: fresh tiramisu is too loose and the flavours are not integrated.

items.name: Digestive biscuit
items.prepNote: use savoiardi sponge fingers, see intro

items.name: Espresso powder
items.prepNote: dissolved in 300 ml boiling water to make strong coffee

content.text: Whisk {{eggs}} egg yolks with {{caster-sugar}} of caster sugar until thick, pale and ribbon-like. Beat in {{mascarpone}} of mascarpone until smooth. In a separate clean bowl, whisk the egg whites to stiff peaks. Fold the whites into the mascarpone mixture in three additions, keeping as much volume as possible.

Mix the espresso made from {{espresso-powder}} of espresso powder with {{fortified-marsala}} of Marsala in a shallow bowl. Dip each sponge finger briefly (1 to 2 seconds per side) and arrange in a single layer in a large dish. Spread half the mascarpone cream over the biscuit layer. Add a second layer of dipped biscuits, then the remaining cream. Smooth the top, dust generously with {{cocoa-powder}} of cocoa powder and refrigerate for at least 8 hours.

items.fix: Dip briefly: 1 to 2 seconds per side. They continue absorbing liquid as the tiramisu chills. Use savoiardi if available.
items.cause: Fingers soaked too long or biscuits not the right type.
items.symptom: Biscuits too soggy, whole pudding watery

items.fix: Fold whites gently in three additions. Work with cold mascarpone straight from the fridge.
items.cause: Egg whites overfolded, or mascarpone too warm.

items.fix: Use sweet Marsala or dark rum. The coffee must be strong, 2 tbsp espresso powder in 300 ml water is the minimum.
items.cause: Marsala substituted for a lesser wine, or not enough espresso.
items.symptom: Tastes flat without the Marsala character

content.text: Tiramisu is one of the most popular desserts in the world, carried out of northern Italy from the 1980s onwards and adapted endlessly since. The original Veneto version is less sweet and more espresso-forward than most British versions; the Marsala is not optional. Made a day ahead and kept cold, it is as good a dessert as any restaurant produces.
